title: SEGMENT 11 — PharmGKB and CPIC: the global resource for pharmacogenomics

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — PharmGKB and CPIC: the global resource for pharmacogenomics
I've been throwing drug-gene pairs at you, with associated guidelines for dosing or drug choice.
Where do these guidelines come from?
Who curates them?
How does a clinician, in the middle of a busy day, look up "patient is a CYP2C19 poor metabolizer and needs an antidepressant, what should I prescribe?
" and get a clear, evidence-based answer?
The answer is two acronyms you need to know: PharmGKB and CPIC.
PharmGKB stands for the Pharmacogenomics Knowledge Base.
It's a project based at Stanford, funded by the NIH, and it serves as the central global repository for the evidence base of pharmacogenomics.
Every published association between a genetic variant and a drug response — every paper, every clinical study, every case report — gets curated into PharmGKB.
Each variant-drug-outcome association is assigned a level of evidence, from one (strong, multiple replicated studies) down to four (preliminary, single study, may or may not hold up).
PharmGKB is, in practice, the Cochrane Reviews of pharmacogenomics.
If you want to know what's known about a particular drug-gene pair — say, fluoxetine and CYP2D6 — you go to PharmGKB and you read the curated summary.
It will tell you what variants matter, in what populations, what the effect sizes are, and what the supporting studies say.
But PharmGKB is a knowledge base.
It tells you what's known.
It doesn't tell you what to do at the bedside.
For that, you need CPIC.
CPIC is the Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium.

It's an international collaboration of clinicians, pharmacologists, geneticists, and informaticians whose explicit mission is to translate pharmacogenomics evidence into actionable clinical guidelines.

CPIC writes the guidelines that say, in plain language: if your patient is a CYP2C19 poor metabolizer and they need treatment for major depression, here is the dosing recommendation, here is the drug choice, here is what to do.
Specific.
Actionable.
Bedside-ready.
As of the mid-2020s, CPIC has published guidelines for approximately 25-30 drug-gene pairs at the highest evidence level, with more in development.
The guidelines are open-access, free to read, hosted at cpicpgx.org, updated regularly as evidence evolves.
They are the gold standard for clinical pharmacogenomics worldwide.
The CPIC guideline structure is consistent across drug-gene pairs.

They give you the metabolizer phenotype categories, the recommended action for each phenotype, the strength of the recommendation, the level of evidence supporting it, and links to the underlying data.
A clinician implementing pharmacogenomics for the first time can essentially treat CPIC as their playbook.
There are also other guidelines bodies — the Dutch Pharmacogenetics Working Group, DPWG, publishes parallel European guidelines that overlap heavily with CPIC.
The FDA maintains a table of pharmacogenomics-relevant drug labels.
In Canada, in Australia, in Japan, in the UK, similar national or regional groups exist.
The guidelines mostly agree with each other, though they sometimes differ in details of dose adjustment or population specifics.
The point is: if you, as a clinician, want to do pharmacogenomics responsibly, the evidence base and the guidelines have been curated for you.
You don't have to read the primary literature.
You go to CPIC, you find the drug, you find the genotype, you find the recommendation.
The work has been done.
That's a profound thing.

Pharmacogenomics has gone from an academic discipline where you had to be a specialist to use it, to a clinical discipline where any prescriber, with a few hours of training, can follow guidelines and improve patient care.
The bottleneck is no longer the knowledge.
The bottleneck is the infrastructure to deliver genotype results to the prescriber at the moment of prescribing.
And that's the next segment.
